justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California mandatory wage transparency via §226, Germany works council approval gate, Indonesia voluntary with contractor-only display. (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard deployment pre-condition (BR-3, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified as the governing factor for display — employee vs. contractor affects deductions, labels, and legal framing (BR-1, BR-5). (4) Formal escalation for legal review present with 4 detailed escalations including legal counsel engagement recommendations. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 through AC-4). (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Excellent jurisdiction-specific analysis with distinct business rules per region, not a single global rule set
Germany works council (Betriebsrat) correctly identified as a hard legal gate with BR-3 and AC-2 enforcing it as a blocking pre-condition
Driver classification difference (employee vs. independent contractor) identified as a core data model concern affecting deduction display (BR-5)
Four formal escalations with affected stakeholders, options, and recommendations — not informal mentions
Edge case for works council revoking approval after go-live shows deep understanding of the German legal dynamic
Edge case for driver relocating from Indonesia to California captures the classification transition risk
Confidence at 52 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas reflecting genuine legal uncertainty
Correctly identified the subtle trap: deduction display mismatched to classification can be used as evidence in reclassification lawsuits (business_impact.risk)
AC-7 adds a safety net for unknown jurisdiction/classification — fail-safe rather than fail-open

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform is building a driver earnings transparency feature that must comply with three distinct legal regimes: California (employee classification under AB5, mandatory wage transparency), Germany (works council co-determination rights over compensation display), and Indonesia (independent contractor classification, no pay transparency mandate). The single feature description does not address how jurisdictional differences affect what is
